speaker: interviewer
text: Paul. You were speaking about this experience of having a chair--

speaker: interviewee
text: Crashed over my head.

speaker: interviewer
text: Crashed over your head.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I can't tell you if they kept me there for two, three, or four days. That is something which I don't know because there was no light. I was downstair 500, 600 feet below ground. It was an old castle, which I understand was 400, 500 years old. And the interview went on for 18, 20 hours in succession.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sleep was terrible. The walls were full of little animals, you know? It was dirty. The toilet was a hole in the ground. You know, the usual thing, which they have-- hasn't changed probably in France. Shouldn't knock the French.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I didn't find in France-- what I didn't find in France was the cooperation of the people. I'd just like to put that in. I was brought before a judge. And my sister Anna must have done something, because the judge said you go back to your hotel. And then he called me over and he said, they're going to follow you. So I went back to the hotel. I went to the front door, got out in the back door, and I went to the Cornish. And I was looking for-- I went into the Hotel Select. And I was staying in Paris Nice, in another hotel. I went to Cornish, and I went into to have a-- meet some-- you know, talking to some French people. But there was no one that helped me. And I went in the middle of the night to this other hotel where Melitta was staying waiting for me. I don't think she knows now how long I stated away, because I asked her that this morning. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, now I'm getting back to the way we left, right? And that was that we left for Perpignan and we went to [INAUDIBLE]. And this is wrong, that word, [INAUDIBLE]. I have to look it up in between. I wrote it down, the word. And it's there on the passport, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they allowed Melitta to stay there because our transit visa was fine. It was a very good printer in the toilet of the Hotel Select. But they told me I needed an inaptitude de service militaire. Surely, inaptitude de service militaire in French, which I had, had to be translated in Spanish. And a stamp from the Spanish consul. There was no Spanish constant in Perpignan. There was one in Pau, which was a little township not far away. And I went to see the consulate in Pau. He looked at that transit visa. He looked at the visa de sortie. And the man said, my God, that is a very, very good forgery. And I said to him, my mother, my father, my brother, my brother-in-law, a child, cousin-- they're all waiting for me, and I haven't heard from them. It all depends if you give me that stamp. The man looked into my eyes. And he gave me the stamp. By giving me the stamp, he legalized the forgery of that Spanish visa. And the visa de sortie. And we arrived. And I went to [INAUDIBLE]. I was away for two nights. And with Melitta we went to Barcelona.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt very bad because I didn't know what happened to my family. I felt better, but then I went the first one, which I saw was a Dutch consul. The Dutch consul, his name was [? Penhause. ?] I told him that my mother, I don't know where she is. He said, listen. He said, there are people here. How old is your mother. I said, my mother was born 1984. He said, that's not old for here. There are here women in prison, or wherever, which are much, much-- 20 years older from the last civil war. The man was not helpful at all. If I say his name was [? Penhause, ?] I'm not sure about it. We stayed in a hotel, which I don't know the name, but I met some people there, Jewish people. They introduced me to [? Mahaus ?]. [? Mahaus ?] were people which were Jews living in Barcelona who were also refugees but which had contacts. I went to the [? Mahaus, ?] I paid them money. And via the [? Mahaus ?] I found out that my mother, my sister, and Paulette were in prison in Figueas. Figueas is written F-I-G-U-E-A-S, Figueas. And that my father, and my brother, and brother-in-law, and my cousin Markuszower were transported to Miranda del Ebro. They were first in imprisoned in Figeuas. And Miranda del Ebro is-- you can't call it concentration camp. It was a camp. It was no holiday resort. They slept four and five on top of each other. If I say on top of, they had these bunkers, you know, on top of each other. It was icy cold there. The food wasn't good. And they had one tap for 200 or 300 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't Auschwitz, or Bergen-Belsen. I don't think that Franco was anti-Jewish. There's even written somewhere which I read that he was a descendent of the Maranos. You know what Maranos are? If this is so, it hasn't been proven, but he was not anti-Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to every consul in Barcelona, and they couldn't do anything for me. The advice was, you go to Madrid. So we took the train to Madrid. By the way, the food, if you had money, was unlimited. There were sausage in the windows. You mention it, everything was available. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we went all night to Madrid. In Madrid, we went again to the consul. And they told us we should see the ambassador. They put us up in a hotel. Hotel is called Hotel Mora. And Dutch government in London is going to pay for that. Why I mentioned it, because I'm coming back to that, all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hotel Maura, again, I went to every consul. I forgot to tell you that I got so desperate in Barcelona that I was sick. I got 41 fever. When I went to Madrid, I felt much better. I thought there it's going to happen. And it didn't happen. And again, I was very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Melitta said it must be from the olive oil, which you are eating, the Spanish food. We go and we buy some mayonnaise. And I said to her, how can you think of mayonnaise if my parents are so far away? And my sister isn't there? And Anna happens to be my closest. Roberta was also close.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot to tell you that the Max Wolf and Bertha, which I found out this morning, and Zeke and Manny left on the 8th of November, Perpignan. The 6th or 8th of November, Perpignan. He also went to [INAUDIBLE]. I will check up that name later on. He was also sent back, but I had Sally Noach waiting for him in Perpignan because I knew it was going to happen. And Sally Noach fixed that up. He came for Lyon to Perpignan. Sally Noach accepted money. Jaquet never accepted money. And he fixed it up in Perpignan. When he went back. He only stayed away either for the day or one or two days, I'm not sure about it. And they arrived straight to Madrid and were in a hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was desperate, and again, Melitta wanted to buy some mayonnaise. So we went to a very large shop at seven o'clock in the evening. Now, you know that in Spain the people work from, say, 10 in the morning until 2. And that goes for the departments, and that goes for the shopkeepers. And then they work again form four or five in the afternoon until 8. So we went to a shop, which was called [INAUDIBLE], and I remember that very well. And I spoke French to the girl behind the counter and she said, hold on a minute. We get madame, who is the owner of this place, to talk to you. So I spoke French to her. And she said, how come that you look the way you look? What's happening with you? And I said, wouldn't you look the way I do when you know that your family is in a concentration camp, and your parents, and my sister, mother, and child are in prison? She said, yes. And then I told her the whole story. It speaks by itself. And I was crying again. She said, can you come back here at eight o'clock tonight? And my brother, who has been the ambassador for France from 1936 to 1939 during the Civil War, he might be able to help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, thank you very much. And I said to Melitta, you know, they're going to help us. There's no doubt about it. This were the first contact which I had with Spanish people, but they were French. The French outside France are much better than the one inside. And at eight o'clock, I went there. I felt already better. At eight o'clock, I went there and there was Monsieur [INAUDIBLE]. And what a fine man he is. You know that aristocracy in Spain, those people they had their real estate, but they also had these shops for whatever reason, I don't know. And this was not a normal shop. You can compare it here with the type of Woolworths in Double Bay. A huge shop with all the beauties which we didn't see in Holland, in France, because we were starving. And he said, I can't help you because the gestapo is everywhere. But tomorrow morning, you go at 11:30 to a shop in the main street. He gave us the name of the street. At 11:00, 11:30, and there will be a man called Leopold Salgado. He's a count, and he was a general in the Blue Division, and he got himself-- Blue Division was fighting with the Germans in Russia. And he will do something for you if you tell him you're coming from me, from [INAUDIBLE]. I knew his first name, but I can't think of it now. Never called each other by the first name. You don't do that in those countries. Naturally, I think this man is God sent. The next day we went to that shop. It was the most beautiful menswear shop. There was no shortage in Spain. There was definitely no shortage in Spain. And there was this unbelievably good-looking man. He was so good looking, you can't imagine. And this man said, just wait. We told him, I'm coming from Monsieur [INAUDIBLE]. Just wait. And he came out. I don't know if I was by myself, or with Melitta. And he took us to a bar. It couldn't have been with Melitta. I think I went by myself, but I'm not sure about it. I think Melitta was with me, but Melitta went, I think, back to the hotel. And he said, we will have something to drink. Must have been 12:00, 12:30. He had a drink with me, and then he ordered pajaritos. And you should know that I was still not kosher, but semi-kosker. Pajaritos are little birds. To eat little birds, for me, was terrible. But this is the man who was going to help us. Not only that he was a count, but he was a general. And I had these pajaritos with him and whatever we had to drink. Was fine with me. And he made some notes. He was not very well organized. And he said, we must first get the women out. The men, that comes later. So, I should meet him the next day in that bar again. I met him four or five times in succession in that bar eating pajaritos, drinking whatever that was, which I don't remember what it was. It was very strong. And the fourth day he said, we can't help them. Because the governor of Barcelona, the Basque land, is not a friend of ours. But I will get these women transferred. He said, as they are on a list with 84 other women-- or, no, 84 including those women. We get them all transferred from the jail in Figueas to the jail in Madrid. I didn't believe that. Three days later, he said they are arriving tomorrow morning. My nerves were gone. And in the Hotel Maura, there were only refugees. So I went to a man who lived in Ebro by the name of [INAUDIBLE] I said to Mr. [INAUDIBLE], my mother, my sister, and another 84 women are arriving, and the baby too. We must do something for them. And my nerves are gone. We need postcards for them. I said, personally I don't believe they will be there. He said six o'clock in the morning, said this, Leopold Salgado, they will arrive. I said I don't believe, but we have to take a risk. They need bread. They need something to eat. They might need milk. He said, [? Marge ?], he said, leave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, here's my money. He said, don't talk about money. And in the morning, at five o'clock, Melitta, myself, and [INAUDIBLE], and somebody else, we went to the station. Believe it or not, 80-odd women arrived with the guard of Seville. You know how the guard of Seville looks like? With their hat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't believe my eyes. They were there. All in a waiting room, organized by Salgado that they shouldn't be pushed in a car. I don't remember if they were chained. But guard of Seville, all around, like to they were criminals. They were allowed to be in that waiting room. We gave them all cards to write their husbands, which were most of them [INAUDIBLE]. We gave them chocolate, which was plentiful available. Anything they wanted, [INAUDIBLE] organized everything. I paid him back later, or even the same day. That's of no importance to that man. He was a diamond merchant from Antwerp. Everybody was kind. And I told them, it's a matter of days and you will all be liberated. They didn't believe me. They went into prison in Madrid. And in that prison was also a Dutch woman, by the name of Mimi Boers. Why I mention that, because I'm coming back to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And within five or six days, were all liberated. He got them out because the governor of Madrid was his friend. Got them all out. From that time onwards, my life became very difficult. Why? Because these women thought that I'm a magician, right? And they were standing in the morning in a queue waiting for me what I can do.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my savior wasn't me. Was this Selgado, Leopoldo Selgado. So now he said, with the pajaritos and the drinks which we had, we are going to take the men out of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, I also met the Dutch ambassador who didn't hear very well. I'd been told later on that, you know, he had one of these old-fashioned hearing places, right? I was told later on that a lot diplomats did that because they want to think if somebody asked them a question what to answer, what to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: This man was old, dilapidated. The consul general, which I think was [INAUDIBLE]. Not I think. I know it was [INAUDIBLE]. His position was a high position, which I spoke to many, many times. He committed suicide a few weeks before the end of the war. He worked together with the Germans. I like to have this on the video. Yes. He started working first to get my brother-in-law out. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No, my father was the first one to be liberated. And my mother was in a hotel around the corner when he came to see her. And my mother was very kosher. She said you can eat ham. You can eat anything just to strengthen yourself, because I was very thin. I'll show you the photos later on. And my father was the first one to come out, and then came out my brother-in-law, then came out Markuszower with my brother was a problem. In the meantime, [INAUDIBLE] told me you have to leave Spain. Salgado said you have to leave because the Germans are going to occupy at any minute. It's full of Gestapos. They are preparing it. And we are already talking of 1943. January, February the Germans had that big offensive in Stalingrad, which they lost. At the same time, they wanted to take Spain and go to Gibraltar. Why they never did that, I don't know. And I'm not a politician. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So where are we? 1943. So my father came out, my brother came-- no, my brother did not come out. Everybody came out but my brother. We told him, Salgado, that my brother has got one leg shorter than the other. He's not capable to serve in the army or anywhere. So he got him transferred to a hospital around Miranda del Ebro. And my brother didn't understand that. He said no. There's nothing wrong with my legs. So when we left Spain, and I'm coming to that, where we left. Then, when we left Spain, my brother were still in Miranda del Ebro, but for some other reason, which I can't find out, my sister-in-law Vita was free. She stayed in a hotel. Not in Maura, in another hotel. How she was free, I didn't find that out. I asked her a few times, and I can't get an answer to that. Now, where are we? So we started organizing. We couldn't get a salida. The same story again. Salida was exit permit to leave Spain. The consul couldn't do anything for me. The ambassador couldn't do anything. No, these people were all stuck in Madrid. Thousands of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Salgado took me to the perfecteur. Over there it's a different name. And we all, the whole family, and all my friends, hundreds of them, they all got salidas. He never took one cent. I met him after the war. He never took one cent. We all got a salida.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we left for Vigo, first my brother-in-law and Berta and the two boys with my mother left for Portugal to go on a boat called [? Niessa ?]. And they went to Suriname. That boat went to Brazil, but they were transferred on the water to go to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Suriname.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left with my father. We left for Vigo, which is Spain, for a boat called We left for Jamaica because with a salida means that we're going to a neutral country. Jamaica wasn't neutral. Was English. The one that was left there-- oh yes, Anna and Issac and Paulette went to Portugal with a Canadian visa, which they got in Madrid or in Portugal, I'm not sure about it. So we were spread all over the world. My brother, after we were on our way to Jamaica, he left for Portugal. And from Portugal to Israel, because he couldn't serve in the army. He really had one leg shorter than the other. We arrived in Jamaica, and my father was in Jamaica. He wanted to go to Curacao. My mother was in Suriname. So I immediately start working towards this. The first thing I did, I volunteered for the army. The Dutch doctor's name was Dr. [INAUDIBLE], a Jewish man. And he said to me-- naturally, my father went with me. And my father spoke to him. There's no need for my son to go in the army. The doctor said, if your son doesn't want to go to the army, I can fix that. Because he lost his son over London. He lost another son-- he had only two sons-- somewhere else. London was the Battle of Britain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, no, I said. I want to join the army. It was arranged that my father should go to Curacao, and my mother-- during wartime this was all very complicated. And my mother would go from Suriname to Curacao that they should be together. Suriname was rather difficult for them. As a matter of fact, Bertha and Max Wolf and also from Suriname to Curacao. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Melitta in the camp. It was a camp, but it was an open camp. It was a camp for refugees, but open. The food was fantastic. It was even arranged that they could have kosher, not kosher. I went to not kosher. My father went for kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were interrogated by an English intelligence serviceman who asked us how we got out of Holland. And why did he ask us these questions? Because even with Jewish people, they were worried that there were some spies in between.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as a matter of fact, they found a spy who was with us on the boat-- a Jewish man-- who was a cousin of the chief rabbi of The Hague who was jailed in Jamaica, and sent to England. And he finished up in the Isle of Man. What happened to him later on-- I know the name of the man, but I don't want to put that in the video. What happened to him later on, I don't know. We have never heard of him anymore. But his wife, Beppi, is in New Zealand, I've been told this morning. And she tells everybody that she did so much for the Jewish people. She wasn't Jewish. And he was a spy. And as far as I remember, on the we didn't trust him. This fellow who took over for me a week before I left for Vigo charged people $100, $200 and $300 per person to get a salida from my contact, from Salgado. And I knew that Salgado didn't take a dollar. Not a dollar. He charged it for himself. This fellow was sent to the Isle of Man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I left Jamaica-- and I mentioned before the name of Mimi Beors. Her husband was Barend Boers who became my best, best friend. And the two of us with another two guys went in to New York. The stopped at least 12 times. It was icy cold because the Dakota was a ride off in England, and had all sorts of holes in it. So we covered ourselves with newspapers. And from New York I went to Washington to go to the State Department to get Melitta out from Jamaica.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed altogether in Jamaica, I think, two months. Could be six to eight weeks. Melitta stayed six months in Jamaica. She got-- it was very difficult because she was born in Berlin, to get a visa for the States. But I went to-- my commander training was in Guelph, Ontario, and I went back to the State Department when I had a weekend off. And I was already in uniform. So that made a lot of difference. And she got an entry permit to go to America. I had my commander training in Guelph, Ontario, which was hard and tough. And I can't tell you how long that lasted. Altogether three months. We were put on a boat, and I don't remember the name of the boat, to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the unit you joined?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the unit that you joined?

speaker: interviewee
text: I joined the Royal Dutch Brigade Princess Irene. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue this tape shortly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take a break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
